intermediate_messages.content:
**Manager Assignment**

**To:** Planner, Researcher, Analyst, Critic  
**From:** Manager  
**Subject:** SP-02 – 12-Week Strategic Launch Plan for AI Course-QA Assistant Pilot

**Task Overview**  
We need a complete, actionable 12-week launch plan for a pilot AI course-QA assistant supporting two undergraduate courses (~300 students total). Team: 1 part-time faculty sponsor, 2 student developers, 1 TA. Budget: $5,000. The assistant must answer only from instructor-approved materials and include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and human escalation. No external evidence or invented policies allowed.

**Subtasks**

**Planner**  
- Draft the 12-week roadmap table (phases, weeks, tasks, owners, deliverables).  
- Ensure all 12 weeks are covered with clear sequencing and dependencies.  
- Identify decision gates at appropriate points (e.g., end of development, pre-launch, mid-pilot).  
- Output: A structured roadmap table with phase labels, week ranges, task descriptions, owner assignments, and deliverable names.

**Researcher**  
- Define the stakeholder map (faculty sponsor, student developers, TA, instructors, students, department decision-makers).  
- Outline the resource and budget allocation plan that totals exactly $5,000 (itemize plausible categories: cloud hosting, LLM API credits, student developer stipends, contingency).  
- Propose success metrics and a data collection plan that respects privacy (e.g., log anonymized usage, survey students, track escalation rates).  
- Output: Stakeholder map, budget table, metrics table with collection method.

**Analyst**  
- Develop the risk register covering hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, low adoption, support burden, and scope creep. For each risk, provide a mitigation strategy.  
- Define the governance and escalation process: when the assistant refuses, cites materials, or routes to humans; who owns content updates; incident review protocol.  
- Create go/no-go decision criteria for expansion after 12 weeks with three outcomes: expand, revise/extend, stop. Include measurable thresholds.  
- Output: Risk register table, governance/escalation process description, decision criteria table.

**Critic**  
- Review all outputs for consistency with the scenario constraints (two courses, 300 students, small team, $5,000 budget, 12 weeks).  
- Check that all required safeguards are present: hallucination (citations, retrieval-only, confidence thresholds), privacy (data minimization, access control, retention assumption), academic integrity (refuse homework/exam answers, policy-sensitive responses), human escalation.  
- Ensure no invented university policies, completed pilot results, or unsupported legal claims.  
- Flag any missing sections from the required output format (10 sections).  
- Output: A critique report with specific issues and suggested fixes.

**Final Synthesis Expectations**  
After receiving all subtask outputs, I will integrate them into a single document following the required 10-section format. The final plan must be coherent, budget-constrained, safeguard-compliant, and end with a recommendation that reflects the go/no-go criteria—not an automatic assumption of expansion.

**Deadline:** Submit subtask outputs within the next cycle. I will synthesize and approve the final direction.

**Planner Subtask Output**

**12-Week Roadmap Table**

| Phase | Weeks | Tasks | Owner | Deliverables |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|--------------|
| **Phase 1: Foundation & Setup** | 1–2 | 1.1 Define scope: identify two courses, collect instructor-approved materials (syllabi, lecture notes, readings, problem sets). 1.2 Set up development environment (version control, cloud account, API keys). 1.3 Draft content ingestion pipeline design. | Faculty Sponsor (1.1), Student Developers (1.2, 1.3) | Approved materials list; dev environment ready; ingestion design doc |
| **Phase 2: Core Development** | 3–5 | 2.1 Build ingestion pipeline: chunk, embed, store approved materials in vector database. 2.2 Implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with citation output. 2.3 Add refusal logic for out-of-scope questions (e.g., homework answers, personal advice). 2.4 Integrate confidence threshold: if retrieval score < 0.7, refuse and escalate. | Student Developers (2.1–2.4) | Working prototype with RAG; citation format; refusal rules; confidence threshold code |
| **Phase 3: Safeguard Integration** | 6–7 | 3.1 Implement privacy safeguards: anonymize logs, restrict access to authorized users only, set data retention to 30 days post-pilot. 3.2 Add academic-integrity filter: detect homework/exam keywords, refuse with escalation message. 3.3 Build escalation channel: route uncertain/sensitive queries to TA and faculty sponsor via email or ticketing system. | Student Developers (3.1–3.3), TA (test escalation flow) | Privacy policy doc; academic-integrity filter; escalation system live |
| **Phase 4: Testing & Refinement** | 8–9 | 4.1 Internal testing: student developers and TA run 50 sample queries per course, check groundedness and refusal accuracy. 4.2 Faculty sponsor reviews all citations for correctness. 4.3 Fix bugs, adjust confidence threshold, refine refusal messages. | Student Developers (4.1, 4.3), Faculty Sponsor (4.2) | Test report; bug fixes; updated threshold; faculty sign-off |
| **Phase 5: Soft Launch & Monitoring** | 10–11 | 5.1 Deploy to one course (150 students) for week 10; monitor usage, escalation rate, latency. 5.2 Collect feedback via anonymous survey at end of week 10. 5.3 If stable, deploy to second course in week 11; continue monitoring. | TA (monitor, survey), Student Developers (deploy), Faculty Sponsor (oversee) | Soft launch report; survey results; monitoring dashboard |
| **Phase 6: Evaluation & Decision Gate** | 12 | 6.1 Analyze all metrics: groundedness, citation accuracy, escalation rate, student satisfaction, TA workload, adoption rate, safety incidents. 6.2 Faculty sponsor leads review meeting with department decision-maker. 6.3 Produce go/no-go recommendation based on pre-defined criteria. | Faculty Sponsor (6.2), TA (6.1), Student Developers

**Researcher Subtask Output**

---

### 3. Stakeholder Map

| Stakeholder | Role & Interest | Influence | Engagement Strategy |
|-------------|----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Faculty Sponsor (part-time)** | Project owner; approves course materials, safeguards, and expansion decision. | High | Weekly 30-min check-in; sign-off on content updates and incident reviews. |
| **Course Instructors (2)** | Provide approved materials; define answer boundaries; receive escalation reports. | High | Bi-weekly sync; material handoff at Week 4; feedback survey at Week 10. |
| **Student Developers (2)** | Build and maintain the assistant; implement retrieval, safeguards, and logging. | Medium | Daily stand-ups; code review by faculty sponsor; access to test environment. |
| **Teaching Assistant (1)** | First-line human escalation; monitors assistant answers; collects student feedback. | Medium | Trained on escalation protocol; weekly log review with faculty sponsor. |
| **Students (~300)** | End users; submit questions; provide adoption and satisfaction data. | Low | Opt-in usage; anonymous survey at Week 10; no direct access to logs. |
| **Department Decision-Makers** | Approve expansion budget and policy; receive final go/no-go recommendation. | High | Receive summary report at Week 12; no operational involvement. |

**Assumption:** All stakeholders have agreed in principle to participate; no formal IRB or legal approval is required for this pilot (consistent with prompt’s “do not invent university policies”).

---

### 5. Resource and Budget Allocation

**Total Budget: $5,000**

| Category | Item | Cost | Justification |
|----------|------|------|---------------|
| Cloud Hosting | LLM API credits (e.g., GPT-4-turbo or equivalent) | $1,500 | ~300 students × 20 queries avg. = 6,000 queries; ~$0.25/query for retrieval-augmented generation. |
| Cloud Hosting | Vector database & app server (e.g., Pinecone + Render) | $800 | 12 weeks of hosting for retrieval index and web interface. |
| Student Developer Stipends | 2 developers × 10 hrs/week × 12 weeks × $15/hr | $1,800 | Capped at $900 each; assumes university hourly rate. |
| TA Stipend Supplement | Additional 5 hrs/week × 12 weeks × $15/hr | $900 | For escalation monitoring and survey administration. |
| Contingency | Unforeseen costs (e.g., extra API usage, domain name) | $0 | Included in above categories; no separate line item. |
| **Total** | | **$5,000** | |

**Assumptions:**  
- LLM API pricing remains stable; no free tier available.  
- Student developers and TA are already paid base wages; this is supplemental.  
- No hardware, software licenses, or marketing costs.

---

### 7. Success Metrics and Data Collection Plan

| Metric | Definition | Target Threshold | Data Collection Method | Privacy Safeguard |
|--------|------------|------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Answer Groundedness** | % of answers that cite at least one approved source. | ≥95% | Automated log analysis; random weekly audit of 50 answers

**Analyst Subtask Output**

---

### 6. Risk Register with Mitigations

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Owner |
|------|------------|--------|------------|-------|
| **Hallucination** – Assistant generates answers not grounded in approved materials | Medium | High | Implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with strict retrieval-only scope; require every answer to cite specific source passages; set confidence threshold (e.g., 0.7) below which assistant refuses and escalates; conduct weekly adversarial testing by TA | Student developers (build), TA (test) |
| **Privacy breach** – Student questions or logs expose PII or are accessed by unauthorized parties | Low | High | Data minimization: log only anonymized question content (strip names/IDs), store logs in access-controlled university cloud storage with retention set to 30 days post-pilot; no student accounts required; developers sign data-handling agreement | Faculty sponsor (policy), student developers (implementation) |
| **Academic integrity violation** – Assistant answers homework/exam questions or generates inappropriate responses | Medium | High | Pre-define a blocklist of question patterns (e.g., “solve problem 5”, “write my essay”); assistant responds with “I cannot answer questions that may violate academic integrity policies. Please consult your instructor or TA.”; all such refusals logged and reviewed weekly by TA | TA (review), student developers (blocklist) |
| **Low adoption** – Students do not use the assistant | Medium | Medium | Announce pilot in class and via LMS; faculty sponsor demonstrates assistant in first lecture; embed assistant link in course syllabus; send weekly usage reminder; offer small incentive (e.g., 1 bonus point for submitting feedback survey) | Faculty sponsor |
| **Support burden** – TA overwhelmed by escalated questions or technical issues | Medium | Medium | Set clear escalation criteria (see Section 8); TA monitors a shared Slack channel for escalations; student developers handle technical bugs within 24 hours; if escalation rate exceeds 20% of total questions, pause pilot for one week to retrain model or expand approved materials | TA, student developers |
| **Scope creep** – Requests to add new courses, features, or materials beyond pilot scope | High | Low | Faculty sponsor acts as gatekeeper; all content additions require written approval from both course instructors; no new courses added during 12-week pilot; feature requests logged for post-pilot evaluation | Faculty sponsor |

---

### 8. Governance and Escalation Process

**Content Governance**
- Only instructor-approved course materials (syllabi, lecture slides, readings, assignment rubrics) are ingested into the assistant’s knowledge base.
- Each course instructor submits materials in a designated shared folder at the start of the pilot. Mid-pilot updates require written approval from both the instructor and faculty sponsor; updates are applied within 48 hours.
- Student developers are responsible for ingesting materials and verifying that no external content is included.

**Assistant Behavior Rules**
- **Answer generation:** Assistant retrieves from approved materials only. Every answer must cite the specific source (e.g., “From Lecture 3, slide 14…”). If no relevant passage is found above the confidence threshold, the assistant responds: “I cannot find an answer in the approved course materials. Please ask your instructor or TA.”
- **Refusal triggers:** Questions that match academic-integrity block

**Critic Report for SP-02 – 12-Week Strategic Launch Plan**

**Review Scope:** Consistency with scenario constraints, completeness of safeguards, adherence to required format, and avoidance of prohibited content.

---

### 1. Missing or Incomplete Required Sections (Format Violations)
- **Section 4 (12-week roadmap):** Must include explicit week-by-week or phase-by-phase coverage. If any week is unaccounted for, this is a violation. Ensure all 12 weeks are labeled.
- **Section 5 (Budget):** Must total exactly $5,000. If a contingency line item is used, it must be quantified and included in the sum. Do not leave budget partially unallocated.
- **Section 7 (Success metrics):** Must include a data collection plan that explains *how* metrics are gathered (e.g., anonymized logs, opt-in surveys) without over-collecting private data. If this is missing or vague, flag it.
- **Section 10 (Final recommendation):** Must not assume expansion. It must be conditional on the go/no-go criteria. If the recommendation is “proceed to expand” without referencing thresholds, this is a violation.

### 2. Unsupported Claims or Invented Content
- **Do not invent university policies** (e.g., “per university data retention policy, logs are kept for 30 days”). Instead, state assumptions: “Assume logs are retained for the pilot duration only, then deleted unless otherwise agreed.”
- **Do not claim completed pilot results** (e.g., “students reported 90% satisfaction”). Only describe *planned* measurement.
- **Do not cite external laws** (e.g., FERPA, GDPR) unless the prompt explicitly provides them. Use generic privacy language: “student data will be minimized and access-controlled.”

### 3. Safeguard Gaps (Critical)
- **Hallucination safeguards:** Must include at least: retrieval-only scope (no generative answers outside retrieved chunks), citations to specific course materials, confidence thresholds below which the assistant refuses, and a fallback to human escalation. If any of these is missing, flag it.
- **Privacy safeguards:** Must include: data minimization (log only what is needed for improvement), access control (only TA and faculty sponsor can view logs), and a retention assumption (e.g., logs deleted after pilot). Do not make legal guarantees.
- **Academic-integrity safeguards:** Must include: refusal to answer homework/exam questions directly, policy-sensitive responses (e.g., “I can explain the concept but not solve this specific problem”), and escalation to instructor for integrity disputes.
- **Escalation to human staff:** Must define specific triggers: uncertainty (confidence below threshold), missing material, sensitive issues (e.g., grading disputes), academic-integrity cases. Owner of escalation must be named (TA or faculty sponsor).

### 4. Coordination Risks
- **Team capacity:** Two student developers and one TA are part-time. The roadmap must not assume full-time equivalent effort. Tasks like “build full QA pipeline in week 1-2” are unrealistic. Suggest phased development with minimal viable product (MVP) by week 4-5.
- **Faculty sponsor availability:** Part-time. Content approval must be scheduled in advance. Do not assume same-day turnaround.
- **Budget realism:** $5,000 is tight. Cloud hosting and LLM API credits will

**Manager Final Instructions to Writer**

**Synthesis of Worker Inputs**

The Planner, Researcher, Analyst, and Critic have each produced strong, complementary drafts. The Critic correctly identified several gaps and coordination risks that must be addressed in the final document. Below are my resolved decisions and specific instructions for the Writer.

**Resolved Conflicts & Decisions**

1. **Budget Allocation:** The Researcher's budget totals $5,000 but includes a $0 contingency line. The Critic flagged this as incomplete. **Decision:** Remove the $0 contingency line. Reallocate the $1,800 developer stipend to $1,500 (reducing to $750 each) and the $900 TA supplement to $600. This frees $600 for a contingency line. Final budget: Cloud $1,500 + Vector DB $800 + Developers $1,500 + TA $600 + Contingency $600 = **$5,000 exactly**.

2. **Roadmap Week Coverage:** The Planner's roadmap covers all 12 weeks but the Phase 6 row is cut off. **Decision:** Writer must complete the Phase 6 row with: "6.1 Analyze metrics; 6.2 Faculty sponsor leads review meeting; 6.3 Produce go/no-go recommendation." Owner: Faculty Sponsor (6.2), TA (6.1), Student Developers (support data extraction).

3. **Privacy Language:** The Critic correctly notes that "30-day retention per university policy" invents policy. **Decision:** Change to: "Assume logs are retained for the pilot duration only (12 weeks), then deleted unless otherwise agreed with the faculty sponsor and course instructors."

4. **Academic Integrity Refusal:** The Analyst's blocklist approach is good but the Critic notes it must include "I can explain the concept but not solve this specific problem" as a middle ground. **Decision:** Include both: (a) direct refusal for homework/exam solutions, and (b) concept-explanation option for general questions that don't match blocklist patterns.

5. **Success Metrics Thresholds:** The Researcher's thresholds are reasonable but the Critic notes they must be measurable without over-collecting data. **Decision:** Keep thresholds but add explicit data collection methods: anonymized server logs for groundedness/escalation, opt-in anonymous survey for satisfaction, TA time logs for workload.

**Final Instructions to Writer**

Produce the complete 10-section document following the required format. Use the following specific guidance:

- **Section 1 (Executive Summary):** Summarize the pilot scope, team, budget, and key safeguards. State that the plan ends with a conditional recommendation based on go/no-go criteria.

- **Section 2 (Goals and Non-Goals):** Goals: Build and test a safe, grounded QA assistant for two courses. Non-goals: Full deployment, multi-course expansion, grading, or answering outside approved materials.

- **Section 3 (Stakeholder Map):** Use the Researcher's table exactly as provided.

- **Section 4 (12-Week Roadmap):** Use the Planner's table but complete Phase 6 as instructed above. Ensure all 12 weeks are covered with clear owners and deliverables.

- **Section 5 (Resource and Budget):** Use the revised budget above. Add a note: "Assumes student developers and TA already receive base wages; this is supplemental stipend."

Task ID: SP-02

Category: Strategic Planning

Difficulty: Medium

Tool Requirement: Prohibited

Prompt:
Create a 12-week strategic launch plan for a pilot AI course-QA assistant at a university department.

Scenario: The pilot will support two undergraduate courses with about 300 total students. The team has one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 pilot budget. The assistant can answer questions only from instructor-approved course materials and must include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic-integrity concerns, and escalation to human staff.

The plan should define phases, milestones, owners, success metrics, risks, and decision gates for whether to expand the pilot after 12 weeks.

Do not invent university policies or completed pilot results. Clearly label assumptions, keep the plan within the $5,000 budget, and restrict assistant answers to instructor-approved materials.

10. Final recommendation

**Subject:** SP-02 – 12-Week Strategic Launch Plan for AI Course-QA Assistant Pilot

**Task Overview**  
We need a complete, actionable 12-week launch plan for a pilot AI course-QA assistant supporting two undergraduate courses (~300 students total). Team: 1 part-time faculty sponsor, 2 student developers, 1 TA. Budget: $5,000. The assistant must answer only from instructor-approved materials and include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and human escalation. No external evidence or invented policies allowed.

**Subtasks**

**Planner**  
- Draft the 12-week roadmap table (phases, weeks, tasks, owners, deliverables).  
- Ensure all 12 weeks are covered with clear sequencing and dependencies.  
- Identify decision gates at appropriate points (e.g., end of development, pre-launch, mid-pilot).  
- Output: A structured roadmap table with phase labels, week ranges, task descriptions, owner assignments, and deliverable names.

**Researcher**  
- Define the stakeholder map (faculty sponsor, student developers, TA, instructors, students, department decision-makers).  
- Outline the resource and budget allocation plan that totals exactly $5,000 (itemize plausible categories: cloud hosting, LLM API credits, student developer stipends, contingency).  
- Propose success metrics and a data collection plan that respects privacy (e.g., log anonymized usage, survey students, track escalation rates).  
- Output: Stakeholder map, budget table, metrics table with collection method.

**Analyst**  
- Develop the risk register covering hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, low adoption, support burden, and scope creep. For each risk, provide a mitigation strategy.  
- Define the governance and escalation process: when the assistant refuses, cites materials, or routes to humans; who owns content updates; incident review protocol.  
- Create go/no-go decision criteria for expansion after 12 weeks with three outcomes: expand, revise/extend, stop. Include measurable thresholds.  
- Output: Risk register table, governance/escalation process description, decision criteria table.

**Critic**  
- Review all outputs for consistency with the scenario constraints (two courses, 300 students, small team, $5,000 budget, 12 weeks).  
- Check that all required safeguards are present: hallucination (citations, retrieval-only, confidence thresholds), privacy (data minimization, access control, retention assumption), academic integrity (refuse homework/exam answers, policy-sensitive responses), human escalation.  
- Ensure no invented university policies, completed pilot results, or unsupported legal claims.  
- Flag any missing sections from the required output format (10 sections).  
- Output: A critique report with specific issues and suggested fixes.

**Final Synthesis Expectations**  
After receiving all subtask outputs, I will integrate them into a single document following the required 10-section format. The final plan must be coherent, budget-constrained, safeguard-compliant, and end with a recommendation that reflects the go/no-go criteria—not an automatic assumption of expansion.

**Deadline:** Submit subtask outputs within the next cycle. I will synthesize and approve the final direction.

### 8. Governance and Escalation Process

**Content Governance**
- Only instructor-approved course materials (syllabi, lecture slides, readings, assignment rubrics) are ingested into the assistant’s knowledge base.
- Each course instructor submits materials in a designated shared folder at the start of the pilot. Mid-pilot updates require written approval from both the instructor and faculty sponsor; updates are applied within 48 hours.
- Student developers are responsible for ingesting materials and verifying that no external content is included.

**Assistant Behavior Rules**
- **Answer generation:** Assistant retrieves from approved materials only. Every answer must cite the specific source (e.g., “From Lecture 3, slide 14…”). If no relevant passage is found above the confidence threshold, the assistant responds: “I cannot find an answer in the approved course materials. Please ask your instructor or TA.”
- **Refusal triggers:** Questions that match academic-integrity block

### 2. Unsupported Claims or Invented Content
- **Do not invent university policies** (e.g., “per university data retention policy, logs are kept for 30 days”). Instead, state assumptions: “Assume logs are retained for the pilot duration only, then deleted unless otherwise agreed.”

- **Hallucination safeguards:** Must include at least: retrieval-only scope (no generative answers outside retrieved chunks), citations to specific course materials, confidence thresholds below which the assistant refuses, and a fallback to human escalation. If any of these is missing, flag it.
- **Privacy safeguards:** Must include: data minimization (log only what is needed for improvement), access control (only TA and faculty sponsor can view logs), and a retention assumption (e.g., logs deleted after pilot). Do not make legal guarantees.
- **Academic-integrity safeguards:** Must include: refusal to answer homework/exam questions directly, policy-sensitive responses (e.g., “I can explain the concept but not solve this specific problem”), and escalation to instructor for integrity disputes.
- **Escalation to human staff:** Must define specific triggers: uncertainty (confidence below threshold), missing material, sensitive issues (e.g., grading disputes), academic-integrity cases. Owner of escalation must be named (TA or faculty sponsor).

# 12-Week Strategic Launch Plan: AI Course-QA Assistant Pilot

## 1. Executive Summary

This plan outlines a 12-week pilot to develop, deploy, and evaluate an AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (approximately 300 students total). The assistant will answer questions exclusively from instructor-approved course materials, with built-in safeguards against hallucination, privacy violations, and academic-integrity breaches, plus clear escalation paths to human staff.

The team consists of one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, and one teaching assistant, operating within a $5,000 budget. The pilot follows a phased approach: foundation and setup (weeks 1-2), core development (weeks 3-5), safeguard integration (weeks 6-7), testing and refinement (weeks 8-9), soft launch and monitoring (weeks 10-11), and evaluation with a go/no-go decision gate (week 12). Success metrics include answer groundedness, citation accuracy, escalation rates, student satisfaction, TA workload, adoption rates, latency, and safety incidents.

The plan ends with a conditional recommendation based on pre-defined thresholds for expansion, revision/extension, or stopping the pilot.

## 2. Goals and Non-Goals

**Goals:**
- Build and test a safe, grounded QA assistant for two undergraduate courses
- Restrict all answers to instructor-approved course materials only
- Implement hallucination safeguards (retrieval-only scope, citations, confidence thresholds, refusal behavior)
- Implement privacy safeguards (data minimization, access control, limited retention)
- Implement academic-integrity safeguards (refusal of homework/exam solutions, concept-explanation option)
- Establish clear escalation to human staff for uncertainty, missing materials, sensitive issues, and integrity disputes
- Collect measurable success metrics to inform expansion decisions

**Non-Goals:**
- Full deployment or multi-course expansion during the 12-week pilot
- Grading, assessment, or personalized tutoring
- Answering questions outside instructor-approved materials
- Replacing human teaching assistants or faculty office hours
- Achieving production-level reliability or performance guarantees

| **Teaching Assistant (1)** | First-line human escalation; monitors assistant answers; collects student feedback | Medium | Trained on escalation protocol; weekly log review with faculty sponsor |
| **Students (~300)** | End users; submit questions; provide adoption and satisfaction data | Low | Opt-in usage; anonymous survey at Week 10; no direct access to logs |
| **Department Decision-Makers** | Approve expansion budget and policy; receive final go/no-go recommendation | High | Receive summary report at Week 12; no operational involvement |

**Assumption:** All stakeholders have agreed in principle to participate; no formal IRB or legal approval is required for this pilot.
